change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew from 158 to 1154 (+638%) since c2 — the +638% comes from appending a templated post-hoc-test paragraph. The current variant of that paragraph was introduced by the decider's ISS-1864 fix in commits 7678cc4 (2026-05-06T06:06Z) and 690ed3b (2026-05-06T02:06Z), ~4 hours before this review. Sourced via §1f in-prompt scan fallback because drift-audit.json (generated 2026-05-05T20:52Z) head 3 candidates (WIN-050, WIN-028, WIN-009) were OBE — already re-reviewed since audit. WIN-060 surfaced as next-priority large_rewrite (drift=638%).
our_argument_summary: WIN-060 (Std Model Explains): westward drift of the SAA western cell is well-tracked by WMM2025/IGRF-14/Swarm; explained by core-mantle dynamics; the dome adopts a centuries-old observation as a V51.0 WIN with no derivation of drift rate or direction from dome geometry; same dataset as WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 and overlaps WIN-004/005, so reading three properties (separation, decay rate, drift direction) from one INTERMAGNET/Swarm dataset does not multiply predictive power. The detail_verdict block was substantially expanded since c2 (158→1154 chars) by appending a templated post-hoc-test paragraph — the SAME paragraph that was just rewritten via ISS-1864 to fix a different factual error.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt (V50.6-era — WIN-060 not yet present; Halley/Gellibrand-era SAA observations predate dome model entirely)
raw-text/03-predictions.txt (W022 = SAA western cell *position* threshold; maps to WIN-040, not WIN-060 drift direction)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (live SAA Decay Rate domain — tracks intensity nT/yr, not positional drift)
data/wins.json (current state of our review's claims about this WIN)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Confirmed: no monitoring domain corresponds to 'SAA western cell drift direction.' W022 maps to WIN-040 (static position 'west of 45°W'), the SAA Decay Rate domain tracks intensity, and there is no separate drift-direction or drift-rate check anywhere in the dome's monitoring infrastructure.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring of any kind for this WIN — it is a pure observational adoption.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: No explicit escape clause. The dome offers no quantitative drift-rate prediction (degrees/year) and no acceleration prediction, so any westward drift confirms it and no eastward drift could falsify it on the timescale considered.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: c2's recommendation to change monitoring='hardcoded'→'none' was applied — wins.json now correctly tags this WIN as monitoring='none'. c2's recommendation to add an overlapping-observable clarification sentence was also applied. Two stylistic c2 recommendations (re-leading the finding with the factual observation, and softening 'as predicted' in detail_claim) were not applied; both remain minor.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Confirmed by direct inspection: W022 ('SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated') in raw-text/03-predictions.txt maps to WIN-040 (static position threshold), not WIN-060 (drift direction). The SAA Decay Rate domain in raw-text/08-live-power.txt tracks intensity in nT/yr, not positional drift. No automated check, no monitor.py entry, no live dashboard panel for drift direction.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Westward drift of geomagnetic features is standard geophysics — explained by core-mantle differential rotation since Bullard (1950) and earlier kinematic descriptions; documented in every IGRF generation. The dome relabels the cause as 'aetheric field rotation' but predicts no different numerical drift rate.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The dome adopts a publicly indexed observation from NOAA WMM2025/IGRF-14 — both predate the dome's V51.0. Westward drift was first quantified from Henry Gellibrand's 1635 secular-variation discovery; Halley's 1701 chart series codified it. There is no possibility of a pre-registration of westward drift direction in 2026 because the answer has been public for ~390 years.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site starts from disc_radius=20015 km or H(r) and arrives at a drift-rate prediction. The dome's 'aetheric rotation' mechanism is qualitative — it cannot reproduce or predict the quantitative MHD core-flow inversions (Finlay et al. 2010, 2016) that yield ~0.2°/yr westward drift rates.

holes_found.description: REGRESSION introduced by the ISS-1864 fix in commits 7678cc4 (2026-05-06T06:06Z) and 690ed3b (2026-05-06T02:06Z). The fix correctly replaced the factually-wrong sentence 'the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line' with 'no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite — making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable: there is nothing to timestamp before the data arrives' for the 16 monitoring=none WINs (001, 013, 022, 033, 040, 041, 042, 043, 048, 052, 054, 059, 060, 063, 066, 067). The replacement preserved the leading 'the' from the original sentence ('…the monitor script hardcodes…') but the new sentence begins with 'no monitor.py entry', so the orphan 'the' produces 'the no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all' — broken English. Verbatim git diff confirmation: prior-text 'the monitor script hardcodes…' → current-text 'the no monitor.py entry exists…'. This is reader-visible gibberish on 16 WIN detail panels of a published critique site, and it landed in the last 4 hours. A dome defender screenshotting any of these 16 panels has free credibility ammunition. Regex test required to prevent regression.
holes_found.recommendation: URGENT global edit across all 16 WINs: drop the orphan 'the' OR restore the truncated phrase that originally preceded it. The cleanest fix is the one already used in WIN-028 (which was rewritten manually in the same commit): drop 'Under the dome author's own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"), the' from the start of the sentence, replace with capitalized 'No monitor.py entry exists for this WIN; …'. WIN-028's rewrite ('No monitor.py entry exists for this WIN; there is no script and no timestamped numerical prediction to evaluate') is shorter, grammatical, and reads naturally — replicate that pattern for the other 16. Add tests/grep-no-regressions.sh check: assert no detail_verdict_text contains the literal substring 'the no monitor.py entry exists'.

holes_found.description: Templated post-hoc-test data-source list is irrelevant for this WIN's domain. The current text reads: 'The confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset (IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.)' — for a WIN about SAA westward magnetic drift, IGRF is correct, but ocean-tide gauges and telluric records are not relevant data sources. The same templated list appears in 18 WINs including WIN-002 (Schumann), WIN-024 (Roaring 40s), WIN-066 (NH heat asymmetry) — most of which are not about ocean tides or telluric currents. ISS-1864 explicitly identified this as part of the cleanup needed. A dome defender will ridicule the imprecision: 'why does your post-hoc test for SAA drift mention ocean-tide gauges?' This is templated boilerplate that hasn't been narrowed to the specific WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace the per-WIN data-source list with a single specific source (for WIN-060: 'IGRF-14 / NOAA WMM2025') and let the cross-cutting framing live in Section 3.1b, or (b) remove the parenthetical entirely and let the prose argue from the WIN's actual cited sources. ISS-1864's recommendation already covers this — escalate the 'fix the data-source list per WIN' subtask alongside the orphan-'the' fix.

holes_found.description: The post-hoc paragraph's argument structure is partially circular when applied to a non-monitored WIN. The post-hoc test asks: was the predicted value timestamped before the confirming data was pulled? For a WIN with NO monitor.py entry, the test is genuinely inapplicable — but the paragraph as written says 'making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable: there is nothing to timestamp before the data arrives.' That's correct in spirit but it gives the dome defender an opening: 'You penalize us for not monitoring; but the unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation.' Our framing should be that adopting a 390-year-old observation as a 2026 'prediction' is the disqualifier, not the absence of monitor.py — the absence of monitoring is a symptom, not the cause.
holes_found.recommendation: Reorder the post-hoc paragraph to lead with the public-availability argument ('westward drift has been a published observable for ~390 years; no fresh prediction is possible') and demote the monitor.py-absence point to a secondary mechanical observation. This makes the argument robust to a defender who challenges 'absent monitoring ≠ failed prediction'.

holes_found.description: Numerical loose-end: detail_evidence says 'westward magnetic drift has been documented since Edmund Halley's 17th-century declination surveys.' Halley's Atlantic Chart was 1701 — 18th-century, not 17th-. Halley's *theoretical* paper on declination change was 1692 (17th-century, correct), but the 'declination surveys' specifically were 1698–1700 voyages and the chart was 1701. Strictly, the surveys span both centuries; calling them '17th-century' is loose. Minor and unlikely to be exploited, but a citation-savvy reader could flag it.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'Halley's 17th-century declination surveys' to either 'Halley's 1701 magnetic chart' or 'Halley's late-17th/early-18th-century declination surveys.' The latter is more accurate; the former is more concise. No action required if the prose is otherwise being touched.

holes_found.description: TLDR/detail framing inconsistency: tldr_evidence says 'SAA westward drift is tracked by global magnetic models (WMM2025, IGRF-14) and explained by fluid dynamics in Earth's outer core.' detail_evidence says 'core dynamics and outer magnetosphere coupling.' The TLDR's 'fluid dynamics in Earth's outer core' is correct (this is the dominant explanation per Finlay 2010, 2016). The detail's 'outer magnetosphere coupling' is a less-standard framing — outer magnetosphere coupling affects rapid (sub-decadal) variation, but the centuries-scale westward drift is fundamentally core-flow + core-mantle differential rotation. The detail framing is not wrong but could mislead a careful reader.
holes_found.recommendation: Align detail_evidence to the standard physics framing: 'core flow and core-mantle differential rotation' rather than 'core dynamics and outer magnetosphere coupling.'

stronger_arguments.description: Quantify the dome's prediction gap: standard MHD core-flow inversions (Finlay et al. 2010, 2016; Terra-Nova et al. 2019) yield westward drift rates of ~0.2°/yr for the SAA cells. The dome model offers no equivalent quantitative prediction — no aetheric drift rate, no acceleration, no relation to disc_radius or H(r). Stating this concrete number side-by-side ('Finlay 2016: 0.2°/yr; dome: undefined') makes the asymmetry concrete rather than abstract. Already in c2 stronger_arguments, not yet integrated.
sources: Finlay, C.C. et al., 'International Geomagnetic Reference Field: the eleventh generation', Geophys. J. Int. (2010)
Terra-Nova, F. et al., 'Relating the South Atlantic Anomaly and geomagnetic flux patches at the core-mantle boundary', Phys. Earth Planet. Interiors (2019)

stronger_arguments.description: Publication-precedence chronology: westward drift was first quantitatively documented by Henry Gellibrand (1635) — the discovery of secular variation. Halley's 1701 chart codified the spatial pattern. Modern satellite confirmation (CHAMP, Swarm) is 21st-century. Adding a 391-year-old observation as a 'new V51.0 WIN' in 2026 is post-hoc retrodiction by the operational definition our review uses. Naming Gellibrand makes the 'four centuries' claim non-loose and gives the reader a concrete first-publication anchor.
sources: Gellibrand, H. (1635), 'A Discourse Mathematical on the Variation of the Magneticall Needle' — the first written record of geomagnetic secular variation

kernel_of_truth.description: The westward drift of the SAA western cell is real, well-documented, and ongoing (data/wins.json:WIN-060.detail_evidence). Any cosmological model has to be at least *consistent* with this observation to be in the running, and the dome model is consistent — it does not predict eastward drift, accelerating outward dispersal, or some other dome-specific signature that would conflict with WMM2025 (raw-text/08-live-power.txt). The dome's instinct that 'aetheric rotation produces directional drift' has the right qualitative shape relative to standard core-flow rotation. Acknowledging that consistency is what makes the post-hoc objection bite — the issue is not that the dome got the direction wrong, but that no derivation produces the value.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Consistency with a known observation is necessary but not sufficient for a scientific prediction (data/sections.json:posthoc-standard). The dome model provides no geometric derivation that independently predicts drift rate, direction, or acceleration (data/wins.json:WIN-060.detail_evidence — 'without any derivation from dome geometry'). Standard MHD models derive drift quantitatively from core flow with falsifiable rates (Finlay 2016: ~0.2°/yr). The dome offers a relabeling of the same observed phenomenon as 'aetheric rotation' (data/wins.json:WIN-060.code_analysis.relabels_standard=true) and counts the agreement as a confirmed WIN. Capturing a 391-year-old observation does not constitute predictive power.

deeper_analysis: Two of the three c2 recommendations were applied to wins.json (the structural ones — monitoring tag fix and the overlapping-observable-clarification sentence). The two non-applied ones are stylistic and remain low-priority. The c3 finding that matters here is the REGRESSION: the decider's ISS-1864 fix shipped 4 hours before this review correctly addressed the factually-wrong-claim issue (these monitoring=none WINs don't have monitor scripts, so the prior 'the monitor script hardcodes the target number' sentence was structurally false), but the find-and-replace introduced an orphan 'the' that produces ungrammatical English on 16 WIN detail panels. The fix needs to ship again, immediately, before the workspace-sync job propagates this state widely. Scientific verdict 'Std Model Explains' remains correct: the dome offers no quantitative drift-rate derivation from disc geometry; standard core-flow MHD does. Verdict holds; text needs urgent regression fix at the templating layer.

citations_verified: WMM2025 (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model) — verified URL pattern, NOAA NCEI product page
ESA Swarm (https://earth.esa.int/eogateway/missions/swarm) — verified URL pattern, official mission page
Halley 1701 declination chart — historically attested, though our prose locates it loosely as '17th-century surveys' when it's actually late-17th/early-18th
citations_unchecked: Whether the implicit Finlay 2010/2016 reference (used in c2 stronger_arguments) is integrated yet — checked: it is not in current detail_evidence
INTERMAGNET direct URL — currently mentioned indirectly via 'derived from the INTERMAGNET global observatory network' without a direct link

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender who has read our methodology principles can argue: 'You frame WIN-060 as adopting a 391-year-old observation. But the dome model's contribution is not to invent the observation — it is to embed it in a coherent geometric framework where westward drift is predicted as a *consequence of aetheric rotation around the disc center*. Standard physics has a mechanism (core flow) but no falsifiable a-priori prediction of WHY the rotation goes westward — it is parameterized to match observation. The dome model also matches observation, with a comparably vague mechanism. Calling our adoption "post-hoc" while accepting the standard model's adoption of the same data is asymmetric criticism. Furthermore, your own review treats Section 3.1b as a "legitimate-novelty carve-out" — but you do not apply that carve-out to the dome model's *consistency* with observation. We never claimed to derive 0.2°/year; we claim our model is *not falsified by* westward drift. That is a weaker but legitimate kind of confirmation, and you should grade us on it consistently with how the standard model is graded.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Insert one sentence in detail_verdict_text that distinguishes 'consistency with observation' (which the dome and standard model both have) from 'derivation of observation' (which standard MHD has via core-flow inversion and dome geometry does not). Suggested addition: 'Both the dome model and standard MHD are consistent with westward drift; only standard MHD derives drift rate quantitatively from first principles (Finlay 2016: ~0.2°/yr from CMB flow inversions). Consistency without derivation is the asymmetry, and it is the asymmetry that prevents WIN-060 from counting as predictive power for the dome model.' This preempts the symmetric-treatment objection by spelling out the discriminating dimension.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Cross-checked WIN-004 (SAA Exponential Cell Separation, Std Model Explains) and the SAA group sibling WINs. All consistently adopt: (a) Std Model Explains verdict, (b) common dataset framing (INTERMAGNET/Swarm/IGRF), (c) absence-of-dome-derivation argument, (d) the same 'one observation parsed N ways' framing. No cross-WIN contradictions detected. Note: 14 of 16 WINs sharing the orphan-'the' regression are NOT in the SAA group — the regression is broader than this group. WIN-028 was rewritten cleanly in the same ISS-1864 commit (using 'No monitor.py entry exists for this WIN; …'), establishing a known-good template that the other 16 should be conformed to.

claims_checked: '400+ year observational fact' — Halley's 1701 chart is 325 years old (2026-1701); Gellibrand's 1635 secular variation paper is 391 years old. The specific attribution in the prose is to Halley's 17th-century surveys, which would be 326-328 years. The '400+' claim is loose under the Halley anchor; defensible only if the count starts at Gellibrand 1635 (391 years, rounds to ~400).

Westward drift rate ~0.2°/yr (referenced in c2 stronger_arguments, not in current text) — verified from Finlay et al. (2010, 2016) and Terra-Nova et al. (2019), all giving westward SAA drift in the 0.18-0.25°/yr range. If integrated into the prose, this number is well-supported.
quantitative_verification.computations: node arithmetic on calendar years (2026-1701, 2026-1635); literature spot-check of westward drift rate values from cited papers.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The '400+ years' claim is loose given the prose anchors it specifically to Halley (~325 years). Either change the anchor to Gellibrand (1635) or change '400+ years' to '~300 years' / 'over three centuries'.
